epigraph.text:
The stone the builders rejected has become the 
cornerstone.
epigraph.quote: In mathematics, you don't understand things. You just get used to them.

content.text: {In mathematics, you don't understand things. You just get used to them.}{John von Neumann}

Certain polynomial products appear so frequently that they deserve 
special attention. Recognizing these patterns speeds up computation 
and prepares you for factoring in the next chapter.

content.title: Common Error
content.content: $(a + b)^2 \neq a^2 + b^2$! Don't forget the middle term $2ab$.

content.title: Squaring binomials
content.problem:
Expand using the special product formulas.

1. $(x + 5)^2$

2. $(3x - 4)^2$

3. $(2a + 3b)^2$

content.text:
The identity $(a + b)^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2$ has a beautiful geometric 
interpretation. Imagine a square with side length $(a + b)$:

The total area is $(a + b)^2$, which equals the sum of the four regions: 
$a^2 + ab + ab + b^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2$.

content.content:
$$
 (a + b)(a - b) &= a^2 - ab + ab - b^2 \\
 &= a^2 - b^2
$$
The middle terms cancel!

content.title: Difference of squares
content.problem:
Multiply using the difference of squares pattern.

1. $(x + 7)(x - 7)$

2. $(3a + 2)(3a - 2)$

3. $(5x + 4y)(5x - 4y)$

content.text: Preview: Sum and Difference of Cubes
For completeness, here are two more patterns you will encounter:

content.title: Verifying sum of cubes
content.solution:
$$
 (x + 2)(x^2 - 2x + 4) &= x^3 - 2x^2 + 4x + 2x^2 - 4x + 8 \\
 &= x^3 + 8
$$
Indeed, $x^3 + 8 = x^3 + 2^3$, confirming the sum of cubes pattern.

marginNotes.text:
By wisdom a house is built, and 
through understanding it is established; through knowledge its rooms 
are filled with rare and beautiful treasures.

marginNotes.author: Pierre de Fermat
marginNotes.text:
I have discovered a truly marvelous 
proof of this, which this margin is too narrow to contain.
